command:
gh pr create \
  --draft \
  --title "Add tic-tac-toe web game" \
  --body "$(cat <<'EOF'
## Behavioral Changes
Two people on the same machine open `http://localhost:8000/` in separate tabs and play tic-tac-toe. Each click posts a move to the server; both tabs reflect current state on every interaction. Win and draw conditions display in the UI. Either player can reset via "New Game"; the reset is visible on next interaction in both tabs.

## Problem
The repo had a package skeleton and CI config with no game logic, no HTTP surface, and no UI.

## Solution
Three modules on top of the existing package layout: `game.py` is a pure state machine with no I/O; `server.py` is a stdlib HTTP server holding a single in-memory `Game` instance, serving three endpoints (`/state`, `/move`, `/new_game`) and the static page at `/`; `static/index.html` treats the server as authoritative and holds no local game state. `tictactoe-serve` entry point in `pyproject.toml` starts the server on port 8000.

## Decisions

**Server-authoritative state over client-side logic.** The alternative puts game logic in JavaScript with the server as a persistence layer (or omits the server). That requires WebSockets or client polling to keep two tabs in sync. Keeping `Game` on the server means both tabs fetch `/state` on every interaction; the sync problem disappears without additional infrastructure. Trade-off: a round-trip per move rather than instant local render, negligible at localhost latency.

**Module-level `_game` singleton in `server.py` over dependency injection.** `BaseHTTPRequestHandler` instantiates a new handler per request; injecting a shared `Game` would require a closure factory or subclassing at import time, adding complexity for no gain in a single-process, single-game-state design. Tests handle this by importing `_game` directly and calling `reset()` in the fixture before each ephemeral server bind.
EOF
)" \
  --repo lalit-qventus/tictactoe-godmode-test4 2>&1
